title: ORDER BY Clause
content:
### ORDER BY Clause

The ORDER BY clause sorts the result set in ascending or descending order based on one or more columns.

---

### Basic ORDER BY Syntax

```sql
-- Sort by a single column in ascending order (default)
SELECT name, salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary;

-- Sort in descending order
SELECT name, salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC;

-- Explicit ascending order
SELECT name, salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary ASC;
```

### Sorting by Multiple Columns

List multiple columns separated by commas. The sort priority follows the column order:

```sql
-- Sort by department (ascending), then by salary (descending)
SELECT name, department, salary
FROM employees
ORDER BY department ASC, salary DESC;
```

### Sorting by Column Position

You can reference columns by their position in the SELECT list:

```sql
SELECT name, salary, hire_date
FROM employees
ORDER BY 2 DESC;  -- Sort by the second column (salary)
```

### Sorting and NULL Values

By default, NULL values sort first in ascending order (depending on the database). Use NULLS FIRST or NULLS LAST for explicit control.

```sql
SELECT name, salary
FROM employees
ORDER BY salary DESC NULLS LAST;
```

> **Key Point:** ORDER BY is always the last clause in a SELECT statement (before any LIMIT/OFFSET).

quiz.question: What is the default sort order in ORDER BY?
Can you sort by multiple columns in ORDER BY?
options: No, only one column is allowed
Only if columns have the same data type
